persons.arguments_against: Strongest case against: the bio prints no birth year, Findlay is fairly common (16 officials, 7 biographies), the bio says 'resigned, 1911' yet the stint is still listed in 1912 — could the 1912 rows be a successor of the same name, or the whole stint a namesake? This fails: the biography's office is the precise compound 'Atty.-gen. and col. sec., New Zealand, Nov., 1906', and the stint opens in the very next edition (1907) as 'Attorney-General and Colonial Secretary' — a unique senior pairing, not a generic title. The 1908-09 rows print the full name 'John George Findlay', and the 1912 rows print 'Hon. Sir John George Findlay' with 'K.C.M.G., LL.D.' — exactly the bio's K.C.M.G. (1910) and LL.D., so the 1912 listing is the same man (publication lag after his 1911 resignation, a known pattern), not a successor: a successor would not share the full triple name, the knighthood, and the doctorate. The honorific 'Dr.' in 1907-08 also coheres with the bio's LL.D.

evidence_cited.fact: Atty.-gen. and col. sec., New Zealand, Nov., 1906
Hon. Sir John George Findlay | Minister of Justice | DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE AND PATENT OFFICE | K.C.M.G., LL.D.

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second John George Findlay, K.C.M.G., LL.D., in New Zealand politics in 1906-1912, or a record showing the Attorney-Generalship was held by a different Findlay during the stint years.

notes: Unique compound office (Attorney-General and Colonial Secretary of New Zealand) with appointment date Nov. 1906 immediately preceding the 1907 first listing; full triple-name agreement; and a two-honour fingerprint (K.C.M.G. 1910 + LL.D.) reproduced verbatim in the 1912 rows. The 1912 listing after the bio's 1911 resignation is edition lag, and the stint ends there, consistent with the resignation.
